articles.title: How Immigration Policies Like Birthright Citizenship Shaped the Supreme Court Justices’ Histories
articles.summary: As the justices prepare to hear a landmark case about birthright citizenship, their family stories are a reminder that the law has shaped who can be an American.

articles.title: Judge Approves Trump Effort to Obtain List of Jews From University of Pennsylvania
articles.summary: The government’s effort to collect the names and phone numbers of Jewish people on campus as it investigates antisemitism has upset some people who worry about how the information will be used.

articles.title: Federal judge finds Trump violated free speech by ordering NPR defunded
articles.summary: A U.S. District Court judge found that President Trump's executive ordering the defunding of NPR and PBS violated the First Amendment.

articles.title: Trump’s Executive Order on NPR and PBS Is Unconstitutional, Judge Rules
articles.summary: The ruling will likely have minimal effect on the federal money going to public media because Congress voted to claw back funding.

articles.title: Trump shares a look at his future presidential library. Here's what to know about it
articles.summary: Trump posted the first architectural renderings of his future presidential library, planned for a prime plot of land donated by Miami Dade College.

articles.title: Trump Redefines ‘Regime Change’ in Iran War
articles.summary: President Trump and his aides have made contradictory statements on whether the United States and Israel have transformed the Iranian government through violence.

articles.title: The wellness world is eager for RFK Jr.'s promised move on peptides
articles.summary: A month ago, Health Secretary Kennedy said his agency would soon give compounding pharmacies the greenlight to make the products, which have exploded in popularity despite a lack of data.

articles.title: Supreme Court Rejects Colorado Law Banning ‘Conversion Therapy’ for L.G.B.T.Q. Minors
articles.summary: The state and more than 20 others restrict therapists from trying to change the gender identity or sexual orientation of clients under 18.

articles.title: ‘The Book of Mormon’ Is Sorry if You Were Offended for 15 Years
articles.summary: The taboo-busting, gasp-inducing Broadway musical comedy has been a hit with audiences and critics. But could it be produced today?

articles.title: One of the first people known to change their gender was an ancient Egyptian pharaoh
articles.summary: NPR art director and illustrator Jackie Lay tells the story of Hatshepsut, who is widely regarded as one of the greatest pharaohs in Egypt's history — but whose legacy was erased for over 3,000 years.

articles.title: Supreme Court strikes Colorado ban on conversion therapy
articles.summary: The Supreme Court has ruled that Colorado's law banning conversion therapy "regulates speech based on viewpoint."

articles.title: Domino, the warty frogfish, is the first of its kind to be raised in captivity
articles.summary: Scientists say the little fish may hold broader lessons for raising other marine species in captivity.

articles.title: Pentagon Weighs Using Anti-Drone Lasers Near D.C. Military Base
articles.summary: Sightings of drones around Fort McNair, the Army base where Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Secretary of State Marco Rubio live, have prompted consideration of deploying the new technology.

articles.title: Gas crosses $4 a gallon in the U.S. for the first time in 3 years
articles.summary: The war with Iran has driven up gas prices at a time when affordability is high on people's minds.

articles.title: 6 books named finalists for the 2026 International Booker Prize
articles.summary: The shortlisted titles include novels and novellas from authors and translators spanning four continents, with stories that range from Japanese-controlled 1930s Taiwan to the streets of Tehran in 1979

articles.title: Will $4 Gas Hurt Trump’s Approval Ratings? Here’s What History Shows.
articles.summary: Presidents since at least the Carter administration have seen their approval ratings tied to gas prices. But there are signs the correlation may be weakening.

articles.title: DHS resumes asylum decisions. And, Iran's strike injures over 12 U.S. personnel
articles.summary: The Department of Homeland Security has lifted its ban on reviewing asylum applications. And, NPR has confirmed that an Iranian strike injured over a dozen U.S. personnel.

articles.title: Iran war delays delivery of medical goods to other parts of the world
articles.summary: The war in Iran has slowed down international shipping, much of which contains medical and humanitarian goods destined for Asia and Africa.

articles.title: A U.S. journalist is kidnapped in Iraq
articles.summary: The Al-Monitor news site identified the kidnapped journalist as Shelly Kittleson, a freelancer who has contributed to the publication. Iraqi security forces say they have arrested a kidnapper.

articles.title: Russia is building in parts of Ukraine. What does it mean for a peace deal?
articles.summary: Russia is heavily developing occupied territory in Ukraine, suggesting it has no plans to return the land as part of any peace deal. NPR's Leila Fadel speaks with Reuters reporter Mari Saito.

articles.title: Figure skating season ends with redemption and heartbreak. What do fans watch next?
articles.summary: Worlds marks the last competition of the 2025-2026 season. Skaters have some time to go on tour, rest up and learn new routines before the next season starts in July.

articles.title: TSA workers may receive pay soon. And, Israel plans to expand its invasion of Lebanon
articles.summary: TSA workers have now been without pay for more than 40 days, as Trump says he has a plan to pay them. And, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu announces plans to expand the invasion of Lebanon.

articles.title: Dominion still has pending lawsuits against election deniers such as Rudy Giuliani and Sidney Powell
